system_prompt:
Summarize this GitHub issue or pull request. Your summary will be embedded as a vector and used for duplicate/similar-issue detection via clustering.

IGNORE and strip away:
- Issue/PR template sections (### Bug type, ### Steps to reproduce, ### Expected behavior, ### Checklist, etc.)
- Testing instructions, QA notes, deployment steps
- Checkbox lists and checklists
- Version numbers, OS details, and environment info (unless they ARE the bug)
- "Related issues" or "References" sections
- Auto-generated content

FOCUS on extracting:
- The core problem or feature being described
- Specific technical components, APIs, or subsystems affected
- The root cause or proposed solution (if stated)

Return concise JSON with keys problem_summary, solution_summary, maintainer_signal_summary, dedupe_summary. Each field: plain text, no markdown, 1-3 sentences. The dedupe_summary should capture the essential topic in a way that would match other reports of the same issue.

results.title: Title channels.discord.proxy does not proxy REST API requests — guild resolve fails, guild channels broken behind HTTP proxy

summary.problem_summary: channels.discord.proxy only applies to the Discord WebSocket Gateway connection; REST API calls during startup (deploy commands, fetch bot identity) and guild resolution do not use the configured HTTP proxy, causing fetch timeouts and guild channels to be non-functional behind a proxy while DMs still work.
summary.solution_summary: Ensure channels.discord.proxy (or a global proxy setting) configures an undici ProxyAgent or setGlobalDispatcher for all Discord REST requests and the guild-resolve code path during provider startup; consider adding retry logic for guild resolve.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Regression with reproduction steps and logs included: WebSocket connects via proxy but REST calls fail with "fetch failed" after ~10s. Workaround using NODE_OPTIONS to set a ProxyAgent fixes REST but guild resolve remains unresolved; standalone CLI resolve works when ProxyAgent is applied. Affected in proxy-required environments; suggested fix is to propagate proxy to undici dispatcher for all Discord HTTP calls.
summary.dedupe_summary: Discord channel proxy only proxies Gateway websocket, not REST API or guild resolve, causing startup REST failures and non-functional guild channels behind HTTP proxies.

judge.reasoning: Concise, technical summary that clearly states the core bug (proxying only the Gateway websocket, not REST/guild resolve) and the impact (startup REST failures and broken guild channels behind HTTP proxies).

results.title: [Bug]:channels.discord.proxy doesn’t apply to outbound REST sends / Carbon RequestClient fetch has no proxy support

summary.problem_summary: Outbound Discord REST requests (sending messages/attachments) fail with "fetch failed" while the gateway WebSocket works via proxy. The channels.discord.proxy config is not applied to REST sends because the Carbon RequestClient uses fetch without proxy support, so REST calls bypass the configured proxy in restricted networks.
summary.solution_summary: Modify the Carbon RequestClient or its fetch usage to support proxies and honor channels.discord.proxy (e.g., use proxy-aware HTTP client or add HTTP/SOCKS/TUN proxy agent) so all REST requests, uploads and downloads route through the configured proxy.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: User in a restricted region reproduces the issue with channels.discord.proxy set to http://127.0.0.1:7890/; logs show REST proxy enabled but outgoing REST requests still fail with TypeError: fetch failed. This prevents bots from sending messages and is high-impact for proxied environments.
summary.dedupe_summary: channels.discord.proxy not applied to Discord REST API calls — Carbon RequestClient/fetch lacks proxy support, causing outgoing message/attachment sends to fail behind proxies.

judge.reasoning: Concise, focused summary stating that channels.discord.proxy isn't applied to REST calls because the Carbon RequestClient/fetch lacks proxy support, which directly explains the outgoing-send failures behind a proxy.

results.title: Discord can log in behind proxy, but native command deploy / bot identity fetch fail with 'fetch failed'

summary.problem_summary: When OpenClaw is configured with an HTTP proxy for Discord, the Discord provider can log in and open the gateway but some startup REST calls (native command deployment and bot identity fetch) consistently fail with "fetch failed" while other Discord requests (application ID fetch, gateway) succeed.
summary.solution_summary: Investigate and fix inconsistent proxy usage in the Discord provider so all REST calls use the same proxy-aware fetch/dispatcher as the gateway/login path; update transport or dispatcher code paths to ensure proxy handling is applied uniformly for startup REST endpoints.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: User provided logs, environment details (proxy configured via channels.discord.proxy), and repeated reproducible failures across restarts; offered to provide more environment details or test patches. The issue appears specific to OpenClaw's Discord provider rather than token/config errors.
summary.dedupe_summary: Discord provider behind HTTP proxy: gateway connects but some REST startup calls (deploy commands, fetch bot identity) fail with 'fetch failed' due to inconsistent proxy handling.

judge.reasoning: Concise, specific summary that captures the core failure (Discord gateway connects but REST startup calls like command deploy and bot identity fetch fail behind an HTTP proxy) without boilerplate or noise.

results.title: [Bug]: Discord proxy config recognized but REST API fails with fetch failed

summary.problem_summary: OpenClaw logs show Discord proxy as enabled but all Discord REST calls fail with "fetch failed" and the gateway websocket never establishes under WSL2 using an HTTP proxy, despite curl via the same proxy succeeding. The Discord channel is unusable: REST deploy and identity fetches fail and gatewayConnected remains false.
summary.solution_summary: Ensure the configured HTTP proxy is actually applied to the Discord.js REST/fetch client and gateway (e.g., pass a proxy/agent to discord.js or honor HTTP(S)_PROXY environment variables) so REST requests and WebSocket connections are routed through the proxy.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Reproducible 100% under WSL2 with provided config (~/.openclaw/openclaw.json) and logs showing "discord: rest proxy enabled" but subsequent fetch failures; Feishu channel works and workaround is TUN-mode transparent proxy. Severity: high; affects any environment requiring an HTTP proxy for Discord.
summary.dedupe_summary: Discord proxy logged as enabled but discord.js REST fetch fails (fetch failed) and gateway websocket never connects under WSL2 — proxy not actually applied to HTTP client.

judge.reasoning: Summary captures the core bug that the Discord proxy is logged as enabled but REST requests fail because the HTTP client isn't using the proxy; it adds a possibly extraneous claim about the gateway websocket not connecting, so minor noise.

results.title: Discord REST API ignores proxy configuration

summary.problem_summary: OpenClaw's Discord channel ignores the configured HTTP(S) proxy for REST API calls: incoming WebSocket messages work, but attempts to send messages fail with TypeError: fetch failed despite proxy set in channels.discord.proxy and HTTP(S)_PROXY env vars.
summary.solution_summary: Route Discord REST API calls through the same proxy-aware SSRF guard/shared fetch path used for web tools (web_search/web_fetch) so HTTP_PROXY/HTTPS_PROXY are respected by Discord REST requests.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Logs show "discord final reply failed: TypeError: fetch failed" while curl via the same proxy to the Discord API succeeds; Telegram and Discord WebSocket respect the proxy; changelog notes a proxy fix for web tools that did not include Discord REST calls.
summary.dedupe_summary: OpenClaw Discord REST API ignores configured HTTP(S) proxy, causing fetch failed errors when sending messages.

judge.reasoning: Concise and specific: states that OpenClaw's Discord REST client ignores the configured HTTP(S) proxy and results in fetch failures when sending messages, with no template noise.

results.title: [Bug] v2026.3.11: CLI 'openclaw agent --channel discord --to' routes to agent:*:main instead of live channel session

summary.problem_summary: The CLI command openclaw agent --channel discord --to resolves deliveries to agent:*:main instead of the live Discord channel/thread session because the session resolver does not search by delivery context (channel + to + accountId) and Discord targets are not normalized.
summary.solution_summary: A manual patch to compiled dist files added delivery-context-based session resolution, normalized Discord targets, fixed lookup logic in the compact resolver, and made the command path pass channel and replyAccount/accountId; recommended fix is to implement these changes in source and add revalidation tests.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: High-regression-risk issue because the fix was applied to compiled dist files and will be lost on updates; maintainers should port the patch to source, add tests to cover delivery-context session lookup, and revalidate post-update behavior.
summary.dedupe_summary: openclaw CLI routes Discord targets to agent:*:main instead of resolving session by delivery context (channel+to+accountId)

judge.reasoning: Concise, specific summary that captures the core bug—CLI routes Discord deliveries to agent:*:main instead of resolving sessions by delivery context (channel+to+accountId) with no extraneous boilerplate.

results.title: [Bug]: Discord threads - volatile metadata re-injection + CLI routing to wrong session

summary.problem_summary: Two regressions: (1) in Discord threads, volatile metadata (message_id, reply_to_id, timestamp, Thread starter, Chat history since last reply) was being re-injected every turn causing progressive context bloat and degradation; (2) the CLI agent command resolver routed Discord deliveries to the default agent:*:main session instead of the live thread-bound session because it didn’t resolve by delivery context (channel+to+accountId). Affected runtime bundles and the gateway CLI session resolver.
summary.solution_summary: Patched compiled runtime bundles to stop injecting the volatile Discord metadata every turn, added session resolution by normalized delivery target in the gateway CLI, added delivery-context lookup in the agent command resolver, and passed channel and replyAccount/accountId into the resolver; also fixed an incorrect resolveAgentMainSessionKey usage. Verified by restarting the gateway service and observing correct writer/reasoning sessions and no repeated context.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Fixes were applied directly to compiled bundles in openclaw/dist/ (dist/pi-embedded-…, dist/gateway-cli-…, dist/compact-…, dist/register.agent-…), so changes are manual and will be lost on reinstall; maintainers should reapply or add a persistent patch/script post-update. Service restart confirmed resolution.
summary.dedupe_summary: Discord thread handling: volatile metadata re-injected every turn causing context bloat and CLI/gateway routing not resolving sessions by delivery context, causing commands to land in main instead of thread session.

judge.reasoning: Concise, accurate summary that captures both core regressions (volatile metadata re-injection causing context bloat and incorrect session resolution for CLI commands) with no template noise, so it will cluster properly with similar issues.

results.title: [Bug]: tools/sandbox: MEDIA_MAX_BYTES (5 MB) is hardcoded and not user-configurable

summary.problem_summary: The sandbox media staging cap is hardcoded to 5 MB in dist/store-*.js (const MEDIA_MAX_BYTES = 5 * 1024 * 1024), causing files larger than 5 MB (PDFs, PPTX, images) to be rejected or silently fail when copied into a sandboxed agent.
summary.solution_summary: Make the MEDIA_MAX_BYTES value configurable (for example via openclaw.json like tools.media.maxBytes) or otherwise derive it from existing per-type limits so the staging cap can be increased/overridden instead of being hardcoded.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Reporter marks this a regression present since at least v2026.3.7 and still in v2026.3.8, provides a manual sed patch workaround to alter dist/store-*.js, and requests adding configuration support for the staging cap.
summary.dedupe_summary: Hardcoded 5 MB sandbox media staging limit prevents uploading files >5MB; add tools.media.maxBytes config to override MEDIA_MAX_BYTES.

judge.reasoning: Concise and technical: identifies the hardcoded MEDIA_MAX_BYTES 5MB sandbox staging cap and the desired tools.media.maxBytes config override without extraneous boilerplate.

results.title: [Bug]: Discord REST API requests fail with "fetch failed" despite proxy configuration being enabled

summary.problem_summary: Discord provider cannot send REST API requests: every REST call fails with a TypeError 'fetch failed' even though the Discord rest proxy is configured and logs show 'rest proxy enabled'. The Gateway WebSocket works and bot receives messages, and curl requests through the same proxy succeed, so only OpenClaw's Discord REST interactions are broken.
summary.solution_summary: No solution or workaround is provided in the report; the user supplied configuration and logs but did not identify a root cause. The issue appears to be a bug in OpenClaw's Discord REST/proxy integration (root cause not determined).
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Reporter provided config, gateway logs, and reproduction evidence (curl works, Telegram unaffected) and requests a fix; severity unspecified but reproducible for Discord REST operations. Logs show 'fetch failed' errors during command deployment and identity fetch, indicating the REST path is failing despite proxy being enabled.
summary.dedupe_summary: Discord REST API requests in OpenClaw fail with 'fetch failed' when using a configured proxy, while Gateway WebSocket works and direct curl requests via the same proxy succeed.

judge.reasoning: Concise summary that captures the core bug (Discord REST requests in OpenClaw failing with 'fetch failed' despite proxy configuration, while WebSocket and curl via the same proxy work), which is the right signal for deduplication.

results.title: Discord REST API (@buape/carbon) ignores channels.discord.proxy config

summary.problem_summary: Discord REST API calls made by @buape/carbon's RequestClient ignore the channels.discord.proxy setting in openclaw.json, so REST requests (and related fetches like memorySearch and attachment downloads) bypass the configured proxy while the WebSocket gateway respects it. The RequestClient calls globalThis.fetch without a proxy-aware dispatcher and Node.js native fetch does not use HTTP_PROXY/HTTPS_PROXY unless special flags or a patched dispatcher are used.
summary.solution_summary: Have @buape/carbon's REST client accept an optional dispatcher or proxy configuration and have OpenClaw pass channels.discord.proxy to it so REST calls use the same proxy as the WebSocket. Short-term workarounds include setting a global undici dispatcher via a preload script (EnvHttpProxyAgent) rather than using NODE_OPTIONS=--use-env-proxy which breaks WebSockets.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Reporter asks maintainers to update the carbon RequestClient to accept and use a dispatcher/proxy and for OpenClaw to forward channels.discord.proxy. They provide a safe workaround (preloading a global EnvHttpProxyAgent) and warn that using --use-env-proxy breaks WebSocket connections.
summary.dedupe_summary: REST API requests via @buape/carbon ignore channels.discord.proxy because RequestClient uses globalThis.fetch without a proxy dispatcher, causing REST calls and media fetches to bypass configured proxy while WebSocket uses it.

judge.reasoning: Concise, technical summary that captures the core bug: RequestClient uses globalThis.fetch and ignores channels.discord.proxy so REST/media requests bypass the configured proxy while the gateway uses it.

results.title: [Bug]: Performance degradation - context growth + lane blocking under load

summary.problem_summary: Under sustained load with long-running conversations OpenClaw suffers performance degradation from unbounded context token growth (writer sessions ~42k tokens, reasoning ~90k) that increases per-turn latency, Discord lane blocking that causes serialization under load, and typing indicators getting stuck until TTL. Root causes identified are lack of effective context token limits, overly high concurrency saturating lanes, and insufficient event queue timeouts.
summary.solution_summary: Applied configuration changes to reduce concurrency (maxConcurrentSessions, agents.defaults.maxConcurrent, subagents limits), set agents.defaults.contextTokens to 80000, and increased channels.discord.eventQueue.listenerTimeout; restarted the gateway and verified no new "lane wait exceeded" or "typing TTL reached" errors. Also improved an anti-regression patching script to cover multiple hash variants and recommended making limits adaptive or configurable via openclaw config.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Maintainers lowered concurrency limits, added a context token cap, extended the Discord event listener timeout, restarted the gateway, and confirmed absence of new lane/typing errors post-restart, while noting restarts alone don’t fully eliminate cases under sustained load. They also hardened the patching script and suggested exposing/adapting limits rather than manual JSON edits.
summary.dedupe_summary: Performance degradation caused by unbounded context token growth and lane blocking under load, mitigated by capping context tokens, reducing concurrency, and increasing event queue timeouts.

judge.reasoning: Concise, focused summary that captures the core failure modes (unbounded context growth and lane blocking) and the applied mitigations, with no boilerplate or extraneous details, so it will cluster with similar reports.

results.title: [Bug] v2026.3.11: Discord thread context re-injected every turn (context pollution + cache bust)

summary.problem_summary: In OpenClaw v2026.3.11 Discord threads re-inject volatile metadata (message_id, reply_to_id, timestamp, Thread starter, Chat history since last reply) on every conversation turn, causing context contamination, increased token usage, prompt cache busting, and performance degradation. The compiled runtime treats Discord like other platforms but lacks an isNewSession guard to limit injection to the first turn.
summary.solution_summary: Manual patches were applied to compiled files: dist/pi-embedded-D6PpOsxP.js to stop injecting the listed fields, and dist/compact-1mmJ_KWL.js to add an isNewSession guard and replace resolveAgentMainSessionKey with buildAgentMainSessionKey. Recommended follow-up is to apply the fix in source code and add a post-update revalidation script.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: High regression risk because changes were made directly to compiled dist files and will be lost on update; local validation shows restored toolCall sessions and resolved prompt caching issues, but an official source-level patch is required and should be prioritized.
summary.dedupe_summary: Discord threads re-inject volatile metadata each turn (message_id, reply_to_id, timestamp, Thread starter, history), contaminating context and busting prompt cache.

judge.reasoning: Concise, specific summary of the core bug—Discord thread metadata is repeatedly injected each turn (fields listed), causing context pollution and prompt cache loss, with no extraneous boilerplate.

results.title: [Bug] v2026.3.11: Discord thread metadata re-injected every turn (context pollution)

summary.problem_summary: In OpenClaw v2026.3.11 Discord thread messages have volatile metadata (message_id, reply_to_id, timestamp, Thread starter, Chat history since last reply) re-injected on every turn, causing context pollution, token bloat, degraded performance and prompt cache busting. The compiled runtime in dist/pi-embedded-D6PpOsxP.js is treating Discord threads as needing repeated volatile metadata, unlike Slack/iMessage fixes.
summary.solution_summary: A manual patch was applied to dist/pi-embedded-D6PpOsxP.js to avoid injecting those metadata fields after the first turn, restoring prompt caching and reducing token usage; recommended next steps are to implement the fix in source code and add a post-update revalidation script to avoid regression.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: High regression risk because the change was made to compiled files in dist and will be lost on reinstall; requires an official source-level fix, tests/CI to prevent recurrence, and attention to related issues (#41355, #34228, #37047).
summary.dedupe_summary: Discord thread metadata re-injected every message (message_id, reply_to_id, timestamp, thread starter, chat history) causing context pollution and prompt cache busting due to a bug in compiled runtime dist/pi-embedded-D6PpOsxP.js.

judge.reasoning: Concise, specific summary that names the exact metadata re-injected, the impact (context pollution and cache busting), and the compiled runtime file causing it, with no boilerplate or noise.

results.title: [Bug]: Discord threads root fix - runtime patches for context growth + session routing

summary.problem_summary: Two regressions in Discord thread handling: repeated per-turn injection of metadata (message_id, reply_to_id, timestamp, Thread starter, InboundHistory) causing unbounded context growth and latency in long threads, and the session resolver falling back to agent:*:main (wrong session) because CLI/resolver lookup did not consistently use delivery context (channel + to + accountId) and command path lacked sufficient context. The root cause was missing runtime patches in compiled dist/*.js bundles.
summary.solution_summary: Applied manual runtime patches to the affected dist/*.js bundles, restarted the gateway, created automatic backups and an idempotent re-application script, and validated that thread-bound sessions now resolve correctly; recommended non-regression tests (long-thread conversations, 15–30 minute monitoring, smoke tests for latency and context size).
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Gateway restarted and is active; no new errors in logs post-restart; thread-bound sessions resolve correctly and CLI routing now targets the correct session. Note: fixes are manual edits to compiled bundles and must be re-applied after updates using the provided script and backups.
summary.dedupe_summary: Discord threads: per-turn metadata duplication grows context and causes latency, plus session resolver falls back to agent:*:main; patched compiled runtime bundles and added reapply script.

judge.reasoning: Concise summary that captures both core bugs (per-turn metadata growth causing context/latency and session resolver fallback to agent:*:main) and notes the runtime bundle patches and reapply script without extraneous boilerplate.

results.title: [Bug]:  Discord messages not being sent - "fetch failed" despite proxy configuration

summary.problem_summary: OpenClaw receives Discord messages and generates responses but fails to send replies; logs show "final reply failed: TypeError: fetch failed" even though Discord proxy is configured and reported as enabled.
summary.solution_summary: Likely a proxy-handling bug where the configured HTTP proxy is recognized for gateway/rest but not applied to the fetch used for sending reply messages; fix would ensure Discord REST fetch calls respect the configured proxy.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Regression that previously worked, reproducible in DM and group chats; logs show rest and gateway proxy enabled and a fetch failure, while curl through the same proxy succeeds, indicating the issue is in OpenClaw's proxy usage for reply sending.
summary.dedupe_summary: Discord replies fail with "fetch failed" because configured proxy is not being applied to message-sending HTTP requests.

judge.reasoning: Concise, specific summary that captures the core bug (Discord message send fails with 'fetch failed' due to configured proxy not being applied) and contains no template noise.

results.title: [Bug] v2026.3.11: CLI agent --channel discord --to routes to agent:*:main instead of live session

summary.problem_summary: In v2026.3.11 the CLI command openclaw agent --channel discord --to ... routes messages to the fallback session agent:*:main instead of the live Discord channel/thread session because the compiled session resolver in dist/gateway-cli-BjsM6fWb.js does not lookup sessions by delivery context and Discord targets are not normalized.
summary.solution_summary: A local patch to dist/gateway-cli-BjsM6fWb.js normalizes Discord targets (e.g. 148... → channel:148...), performs session lookup by delivery context (channel+to+accountId) before falling back to main, and thereby restores correct CLI routing to the live session.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: High regression risk: this is a manual change to compiled files in dist/ and will be lost on updates; requires a source-level fix and tests to normalize targets and add delivery-context session lookup. Related requests: #36401 and #41562.
summary.dedupe_summary: CLI routing to Discord channels resolves to agent:*:main because session resolver omits delivery-context lookup and Discord target normalization; needs session lookup by channel+to+accountId.

judge.reasoning: Concise, specific summary of the core bug: CLI routes to agent:*:main because the session resolver lacks delivery-context lookup and Discord target normalization, and calls for lookup by channel+to+accountId.

results.title: [Bug] v2026.3.11: Regression in compact - resolveAgentMainSessionKey ReferenceError

summary.problem_summary: After applying an earlier session-resolver patch in v2026.3.11, the compiled file dist/compact-1mmJ_KWL.js calls a non-existent function resolveAgentMainSessionKey, causing ReferenceError at runtime, agent command failures, and potential gateway crashes when processing agent commands.
summary.solution_summary: Locally patched dist/compact-1mmJ_KWL.js to replace resolveAgentMainSessionKey(...) with the correct buildAgentMainSessionKey(...), which removes the ReferenceError and restores agent command and session behavior; note this is a manual change to compiled output and will be lost on reinstall/update.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: High-risk regression introduced by a prior fix; maintainers should apply the correct change in source/build (not just dist) and ensure fix #5 is included whenever fix #3 (session resolver in compact) is applied to avoid ReferenceError.
summary.dedupe_summary: Regression in dist/compact-1mmJ_KWL.js: ReferenceError due to resolveAgentMainSessionKey used instead of buildAgentMainSessionKey, causing agent command failures.

judge.reasoning: Concise and specific: names the compiled file, the incorrect vs correct function, and the resulting ReferenceError/agent failures, with no boilerplate—will cluster well with similar regressions.

results.title: [Bug]:  Discord plugin fails with "fetch failed" when using proxy

summary.problem_summary: Discord channel plugin fails health checks and message sends in proxy-required environments because channels.discord.proxy is only applied to WebSocket connections; REST API (HTTP) calls bypass the proxy and return "fetch failed" while curl with the same proxy succeeds.
summary.solution_summary: Apply channels.discord.proxy to the plugin's HTTP/REST client and health-check requests (or make the HTTP client respect the configured proxy), so REST API calls are routed through the proxy like the WebSocket connections.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: User reports a regression (worked previously) on OpenClaw 2026.3.8 with reproduction steps and confirms the proxy itself works via curl; this impacts CLI message sending and health checks and needs a code change in the discord channel implementation.
summary.dedupe_summary: Discord channel REST API calls ignore channels.discord.proxy causing fetch failed, failing message send and health checks.

judge.reasoning: Concise and focused on the core bug—Discord REST API calls ignore channels.discord.proxy causing fetch failures and related message/health-check errors—so it will cluster with similar reports.

results.title: [Bug] v2026.3.11: Session resolver in compact lacks delivery-context lookup

summary.problem_summary: In OpenClaw v2026.3.11 the session resolver in the agent command inside the compiled bundle dist/compact-1mmJ_KWL.js does not look up sessions by delivery context (channel + to + accountId), causing resolution to fall back to agent:*:main and agent responses to be associated with the wrong session.
summary.solution_summary: User patched dist/compact-1mmJ_KWL.js to add a delivery-context lookup: search for an existing session by channel+to+accountId and only fallback to main when no specific session is found; validation shows correct routing after the change.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: The fix was applied manually to a compiled dist file and carries HIGH regression risk because it will be lost on reinstall/update; the lookup must be implemented in the source/build pipeline and shipped in an upstream release.
summary.dedupe_summary: Compiled bundle (dist/compact-1mmJ_KWL.js) session resolver missing delivery-context (channel+to+accountId) lookup, causing fallback to agent:*:main and incorrect session routing.

judge.reasoning: Concise and specific: identifies the compiled file, the missing delivery-context (channel+to+accountId) lookup, and the resulting fallback to agent:*:main causing incorrect session routing.

results.title: [Feature Request] Add i18n support for OpenClaw Control UI (Chinese localization)

summary.problem_summary: The OpenClaw Control UI has most interface text hardcoded in English (settings, labels, buttons, agent/skill pages, channel configs, status messages, tooltips), creating usability barriers for non-English users; hardcoded strings are present in the distributed control-ui bundle.
summary.solution_summary: Extract UI strings into a structured i18n layer (e.g., JSON locale files), add a zh-CN locale with translations, provide a language selector or auto-detect system language, and build infrastructure to easily add more languages.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Requester flags this as medium-high priority for UX accessibility, notes docs already have zh-CN translations, and asks whether i18n for control-ui is on the roadmap or if maintainers/others want to collaborate on implementation.
summary.dedupe_summary: Control UI lacks localization; request to add i18n support and a Simplified Chinese (zh-CN) locale by extracting hardcoded English strings and adding language selection/auto-detect.

judge.reasoning: Concise summary that captures the core request to add i18n support and a zh-CN locale by extracting hardcoded English strings and adding language selection/auto-detect, with no extraneous boilerplate.

results.title: feat(whatsapp): add systemPrompt support for accounts and groups

summary.problem_summary: WhatsApp group chats could not apply channel-level systemPrompt instructions at account or group granularity, preventing per-account or per-group customization and wildcard defaults for groups without their own prompt.
summary.solution_summary: Adds systemPrompt support to WhatsApp config types/schema and implements resolution and composition logic so the final injected prompt is account systemPrompt plus the resolved group systemPrompt (specific group then '*' wildcard). Account-level groups fully override root groups; includes tests and docs.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Feature PR with tests and documentation; backward compatible and limited to the WhatsApp channel only (no hot-reload, allowlist, or mention-gating changes). Closes the linked issue and replaces a previous broader PR.
summary.dedupe_summary: Add per-account and per-group systemPrompt support for WhatsApp with precedence: account prompt then group prompt (specific -> wildcard); account groups override root groups.

judge.reasoning: Concise, focused summary that states the feature (per-account and per-group WhatsApp systemPrompt) and its precedence rules, matching the core change without boilerplate.

results.title: Fix WhatsApp group append processing and account-scoped gatin

summary.problem_summary: WhatsApp group messages delivered via messages.upsert(type="append") were being dropped before inbound/agent processing, and group policy helpers resolved WhatsApp group policy without accountId so account-scoped groupAllowFrom/allowFrom were ignored, causing allowlist fallback to fail closed.
summary.solution_summary: Enable append processing only for WhatsApp group messages and propagate accountId when resolving WhatsApp group policy so account-scoped groupAllowFrom/allowFrom (and allowlist fallback) are applied; DM append history remains skipped.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Bugfix touching web inbound and group activation code (notably src/web/inbound/monitor.ts and src/web/auto-reply/monitor/group-activation.ts), validated against a live OpenClaw WhatsApp deployment with trace logs and targeted tests; change is backward-compatible and scoped to WhatsApp groups.
summary.dedupe_summary: WhatsApp group append messages skipped and account-scoped group policy ignored, preventing group messages from triggering the agent.

judge.reasoning: Concise and specific: it names the WhatsApp group 'append' handling being skipped and the account-scoped group policy being ignored, which are the core issues needed for correct deduplication.

results.title: fix(gateway): resolve Canvas 401 behind Tailscale Serve

summary.problem_summary: Canvas/A2UI returned 401 when accessed via Tailscale Serve because the gateway failed to resolve the client IP when a trusted proxy sent requests without forwarding headers and the local-request check did not fall back to the socket remote address when allowRealIpFallback was enabled. Additionally, unconditional acceptance of .ts.net Host headers posed a security concern.
summary.solution_summary: resolveClientIp now returns the socket address as a fallback when no forwarding headers are present; isLocalDirectRequest checks req.socket.remoteAddress when allowRealIpFallback is enabled; isLocalishHost now gates .ts.net acceptance behind the existing allowTailscale flag and that flag is threaded through relevant callers. Documentation updated and tests added to verify behavior.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: New tests added (105 passing) and failing logs fixed; PR closes issue #28472 and depends on another PR for overlapping allowTailscale changes. No new config keys introduced, but docs require gateway.trustedProxies and gateway.allowRealIpFallback when using Tailscale Serve; allowTailscale still defaults to true for tailscale.mode="serve" to preserve compatibility.
summary.dedupe_summary: 401 authentication failure for Canvas behind Tailscale Serve due to missing client-IP fallback and local-request detection; fixed by falling back to socket.remoteAddress and gating .ts.net host acceptance behind allowTailscale.

judge.reasoning: Concise, specific summary of the core bug (401 for Canvas behind Tailscale Serve), root causes (missing client-IP/socket fallback and local-request detection), and the precise fixes (fallback to socket.remoteAddress and gating .ts.net via allowTailscale), so it will cluster correctly with similar reports.

results.title: fix(security): block non-self messages in WhatsApp self-chat mode

summary.problem_summary: checkInboundAccessControl computes an isSelfChat value but never uses it as a guard, so non-self WhatsApp messages (groups, DMs from strangers, broadcasts) can pass through because resolveWhatsAppRuntimeGroupPolicy defaults to "open" when cfg.channels.whatsapp exists with no explicit groupPolicy. This violates the intended self-chat isolation boundary and lets non-self messages reach downstream processing.
summary.solution_summary: Add an early-return guard in checkInboundAccessControl that rejects any non-self message when account.selfChatMode === true, placed before group/DM policy evaluation. The guard checks the explicit config flag only (not the heuristic isSelfChat) to avoid blocking teammates allowed via allowFrom; isSelfChat remains computed for UI/logging.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Change limited to src/web/inbound/access-control.ts with tests updated/verified; all 9 related tests pass and code-path analysis confirms the guard fires before policy resolution. The fix is intentionally scoped to explicit selfChatMode and can be reverted by removing the early-return guard; reviewers should note auto-detected self-chat is not hard-blocked by design.
summary.dedupe_summary: WhatsApp self-chat mode not enforcing isolation because isSelfChat was computed but unused, allowing group/other non-self messages through; fixed by enforcing explicit selfChatMode guard in inbound access control.

judge.reasoning: Concise, specific summary that identifies the bug (isSelfChat computed but unused), its effect (non-self WhatsApp messages bypass isolation), and the fix (explicit selfChatMode guard in inbound access control).

results.title: fix: store group messages from non-allowlisted senders as pending context

summary.problem_summary: When groupPolicy is set to "allowlist", group messages from senders not in groupAllowFrom are dropped at access-control and never reach the mention-gating/context-storage logic, so agents cannot see those messages in the "Chat messages since your last reply" context block.
summary.solution_summary: Adds an opt-in flag groupContextFromAll (top-level and per-account, default false). When enabled, access-control returns storeForContext for non-allowlisted senders, messages are marked contextOnly (no read receipts), stored as pending group history, and do not trigger replies; per-account config takes precedence.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: PR is localized and opt-in with zero default behavior change and local verification with two bots; greptile flagged a 3/5 confidence due to potential allowlist normalization/matching bugs and the new context-only path bypassing existing group gating/state updates that may cause subtle inconsistencies.
summary.dedupe_summary: WhatsApp groups: non-allowlisted senders' messages are currently dropped under groupPolicy: allowlist; add groupContextFromAll flag to store those messages as contextOnly instead of discarding.

judge.reasoning: Concise and specific: states that with groupPolicy: allowlist non-allowlisted group messages are dropped and the change adds a groupContextFromAll flag to store them as context-only instead of discarding.

results.title: feat(sessions_spawn): add sessionKey param to reuse sub-agent sessions

summary.problem_summary: Adds an optional sessionKey parameter to sessions_spawn so sub-agents can reuse deterministic sessions instead of creating a new random-UUID each spawn. Short keys are namespaced to agent:{agentId}:subagent:{key}, fully-qualified keys are accepted only if the embedded agentId matches the target agent, and omission falls back to crypto.randomUUID(). Also intended to fix a session key allowlist bypass and includes four unit tests for deterministic and fallback behaviors.
summary.solution_summary: Implements sessionKey handling, namespacing, and cross-agent validation in sessions_spawn, with a fallback to crypto.randomUUID() when sessionKey is omitted; adds tests for deterministic key, UUID fallback, fully-qualified passthrough, and cross-agent mismatch rejection. Note: tests currently reference a non-existent import which prevents compilation.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Automated review flagged blocking issues: a test imports ./openclaw-tools.js which does not exist (should be ./clawdbot-tools.js), a case-sensitivity bug where embedded agentId from fully-qualified keys isn’t normalized against normalizeAgentId(), and inconsistent error handling (throw vs returning jsonResult) for cross-agent mismatches; these must be fixed before merging.
summary.dedupe_summary: Add sessionKey to sessions_spawn to reuse deterministic sub-agent sessions with namespacing and cross-agent validation, plus tests (but test import, case-sensitivity, and error-handling issues reported).

judge.reasoning: Summarizes the core change—adding a deterministic sessionKey to sessions_spawn with namespacing and cross-agent validation—so it will cluster correctly, but it includes noisy test/import/case-sensitivity/error-handling remarks that are not essential for deduplication.

results.title: fix(telegram): auto-detect captionable messages for editMessageCaption

summary.problem_summary: Editing Telegram messages with media (photo, video, audio, document, animation) using the generic edit path calls editMessageText and gets a 400 error "there is no text in the message to edit", causing message(action=edit) to fail for captionable messages and leave the UI inconsistent.
summary.solution_summary: editMessageTelegram in src/telegram/send.ts now detects that specific 400 error via NO_TEXT_IN_MESSAGE_RE, retries with editMessageCaption for captionable messages, and uses editMessageReplyMarkup directly for buttons-only updates; MESSAGE_NOT_MODIFIED is treated as success and HTML parse fallback is preserved.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Six unit tests added covering retry path, buttons-only path, error propagation, and MESSAGE_NOT_MODIFIED handling; change is backward-compatible with no API surface changes, touches only the Telegram adapter file, and carries a small risk if Telegram's error wording changes (regex scoped tightly); not manually end-to-end tested with a live bot.
summary.dedupe_summary: Telegram media messages fail to edit because editMessageText is used on captionable messages; fix is to auto-detect the "no text in message" 400 and retry with editMessageCaption or use editMessageReplyMarkup for buttons-only edits.

judge.reasoning: Concise, specific description of the Telegram editMessageText->400 failure on captionable media and the retry-to-editMessageCaption / use editMessageReplyMarkup fix, which will match similar API-edit bugs.

results.title: fix(whatsapp): restore config-driven block streaming for WhatsApp delivery

summary.problem_summary: A prior change hardcoded disableBlockStreaming: true for WhatsApp, which unintentionally turned off block streaming so messages were batched and sent only when the agent finished, ignoring per-account or global blockStreaming settings.
summary.solution_summary: Restore config-driven behavior: the deliver callback now only suppresses tool payloads (not all payloads) and disableBlockStreaming is derived from channels.whatsapp.blockStreaming or agents.defaults.blockStreamingDefault, while upstream filtering continues to suppress reasoning payloads.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Fix verified with unit/integration tests and live WhatsApp checks; 13 inbound contract tests pass; backward compatible and no security/network changes; risk noted if upstream shouldSuppressReasoningPayload is removed.
summary.dedupe_summary: WhatsApp block streaming was disabled by a hardcoded flag; this PR makes block streaming respect per-account and global config and only suppresses tool/reasoning payloads appropriately.

judge.reasoning: Concise, technical summary that captures the core change—removing a hardcoded disable for WhatsApp block streaming and making suppression limited to tool/reasoning payloads while honoring per-account/global config—without extraneous boilerplate.

results.title: fix(telegram): fix gateway hang on video messages and increase media download limit

summary.problem_summary: Sending video media via the Telegram channel could make the gateway hang and become unresponsive because a content-length check failed before the response body was consumed, causing undici Agent.close() (closeDispatcher) to deadlock; the old default mediaMaxMb (5MB) also caused otherwise-valid files to be rejected.
summary.solution_summary: Raised default channels.telegram.mediaMaxMb from 5 to 20, fixed closeDispatcher to timeout and fall back to destroy to avoid Agent.close deadlocks, made media download errors non-fatal so captions still forward, and added timeouts and retry logic to getFile and file downloads; also extended inbound metadata stripping for UI.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Changes are backward compatible, no new dependencies, and configurable via channels.telegram.mediaMaxMb; tests updated (strip-inbound-meta.test.ts passing), verified end-to-end with a 7.5MB video, and key files touched include src/infra/net/ssrf.ts and src/telegram/bot.ts; risk noted for the 3s dispatcher timeout but mitigations are documented.
summary.dedupe_summary: Telegram gateway hangs on video uploads due to unconsumed response body causing undici Agent.close deadlock and strict 5MB media limit; fix increases media limit and adds dispatcher timeout/retries to prevent hangs.

judge.reasoning: Concise, technical summary capturing the root cause (undici Agent.close deadlock from unconsumed response body), symptom (gateway hangs on video uploads), and the key fixes (increase media limit, add timeouts/retries), with no boilerplate noise.

results.title: fix(telegram): use type-aware media placeholder for document attachments (#7116)

summary.problem_summary: Telegram inbound body fallback always used the hardcoded "<media:image>" when a message had media but no caption, so document, video, and other non-photo attachments were misidentified to the agent (e.g., PDFs appeared as images).
summary.solution_summary: Change resolveTelegramInboundBody to use the already-computed type-aware placeholder variable (producing <media:document>, <media:video>, etc.) for the fallback body text and add unit tests to cover media placeholder behavior and document message handling; media download and extraction logic remain unchanged.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: This is a small, scoped bugfix in the Telegram integration that updates only the agent-facing body placeholder; new unit tests were added in extensions/telegram/src/bot/helpers.test.ts and extensions/telegram/src/bot.create-telegram-bot.test.ts to prevent regressions; closes #7116.
summary.dedupe_summary: Telegram integration mislabels non-photo media as <media:image> in empty-body fallback instead of using a type-aware <media:document>/<media:video> placeholder.

judge.reasoning: Concise, specific description of the Telegram integration bug (wrong <media:image> fallback vs type-aware <media:document>/<media:video>), with no boilerplate—suitable for deduplication.

results.title: media: make MEDIA_MAX_BYTES configurable via media.maxBytes

summary.problem_summary: MEDIA_MAX_BYTES was hardcoded to 5 MB in src/media/store.ts so files larger than 5 MB silently failed to stage into sandboxed agents, preventing common file types (PDFs, presentations, high-res images) from being processed.
summary.solution_summary: Introduced a new media.maxBytes configuration field, replaced the hardcoded constant with getMediaMaxBytes()/setMediaMaxBytes() accessors, and wired gateway boot to read the config (defaulting to 5 MB). Schema caps values at 500 MB and error messages now show the actual configured limit.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: All 56 unit tests pass (store, server, staging, schema tests); bot review comments were addressed (reset behavior, MB formatting); change is backward compatible (default preserved, deprecated constant exported) and reversible by removing the config key; risks mitigated by schema max and TTL cleanup.
summary.dedupe_summary: Make media staging size limit configurable via media.maxBytes (override default 5 MB) to allow staging of larger files into sandboxed agents.

judge.reasoning: Concise and focused: states the core change (make media.maxBytes configurable replacing hardcoded 5 MB limit) using specific technical terms and no template noise.

results.title: fix: pass file metadata to agent when Telegram media download fails

summary.problem_summary: When Telegram media download fails (due to exceeding configured mediaMaxMb or Telegram API errors), the handler returned early and silently dropped the entire message so the agent never saw it, received no ack reaction, and the user got no indication. This affects the Telegram integration message pipeline in src/telegram/bot-handlers.ts and processMessage dispatching.
summary.solution_summary: On download failure, extract file metadata (name, size, type) from the Telegram message object, build a metadata stub and inject it into msg.text, and let the message continue through the normal pipeline instead of returning early; previous automatic bot replies for these errors are removed so the agent can handle responses.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Bug fix targeting the Telegram integration (src/telegram/bot-handlers.ts). Backward compatible, low risk, reversible by reverting the single commit; note the change mutates msg.text only in the error path where messages previously were dropped.
summary.dedupe_summary: Telegram media download failures cause silent message drops; inject file metadata into message text and continue processing to avoid loss.

judge.reasoning: Concise and specific: identifies the root cause (silent drops when Telegram media download fails) and the implemented fix (inject file metadata into message text and let the message continue), which is ideal for deduplication.

results.title: feat(cli): add --session-key targeting for openclaw agent

summary.problem_summary: The openclaw agent CLI lacked a --session-key option, so CLI-triggered deliveries in multi-channel/multi-agent setups could resolve to the agent main session instead of an intended stable channel session because sessionId is UUID-based and external callers often can only supply a stable session key or lack a sender-derived --to.
summary.solution_summary: Adds a first-class --session-key flag to the openclaw agent command, surfaces sessionKey in AgentCliOpts and forwards it to resolveSessionKeyForRequest/gateway, updates help/validation text, keeps resolution precedence (sessionKey > sessionId > to > agent), and adds tests verifying CLI parsing and gateway forwarding.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Tests added for CLI parsing and gateway forwarding and are passing; change is additive and backward-compatible, small risk, follows existing resolver semantics and ready to merge.
summary.dedupe_summary: Add explicit --session-key support to openclaw agent CLI to target stable session keys for message/callback routing.

judge.reasoning: Concise and specific: states the CLI change (--session-key), target command (openclaw agent), and intent (use stable session keys for message/callback routing), with no boilerplate or noisy details.

results.title: feat: separate group-level allowlist from sender-level command authorization

summary.problem_summary: With groupPolicy: "allowlist" the current logic gates all group interactions (chat and slash commands) by sender phone number via groupAllowFrom, making it impossible to allowlist groups by JID, keep chat open to all participants in approved groups, or restrict slash commands to owners while preventing spam groups.
summary.solution_summary: When groupPolicy: "allowlist" and a channels.whatsapp.groups mapping exists, the access control now uses the groups mapping (group JIDs) as the group-level gate so approved groups accept messages from any participant while groupAllowFrom still controls slash command authorization; when no groups mapping is present, the legacy sender-based behavior is preserved.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Code changes update src/web/inbound/access-control.ts to call resolveChannelGroupPolicy, update JSDoc in src/config/types.whatsapp.ts, and add a Vitest suite (4 tests) covering allowlist behavior; this PR depends on #3326 and preserves backward compatibility, with one minor stale-comment doc fix noted.
summary.dedupe_summary: Separate group-level allowlist by group JID from sender-level command authorization: when a groups mapping exists, gate group membership by JID and keep slash commands restricted by groupAllowFrom.

judge.reasoning: Concise, specific summary that captures the core change—use groups JID mapping for group-level allowlist while preserving sender-based command authorization via groupAllowFrom—without noisy boilerplate.

results.title: fix(telegram): propagate mediaMaxMb limit to outbound media reply delivery

summary.problem_summary: Configured mediaMaxMb for Telegram is ignored when delivering final reply messages containing media because loadWebMedia in the reply delivery path receives no maxBytes and falls back to the 100 MB default, while the direct-send path already honored the setting.
summary.solution_summary: Thread mediaMaxBytes through the Telegram outbound reply delivery chain (bot.ts → bot-message.ts → bot-message-dispatch.ts → delivery.replies.ts and bot-native-commands.ts) so loadWebMedia receives and enforces the configured maxBytes; added unit tests verifying behavior and preserved default when unset.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Change is a small, backward-compatible bug fix limited to the Telegram integration, includes two unit tests and all related suites passing (108 tests), no security or API changes, and closes the linked issue.
summary.dedupe_summary: Telegram outbound reply media ignores mediaMaxMb (loadWebMedia lacked maxBytes); fix by passing mediaMaxBytes through outbound delivery chain to enforce limit.

judge.reasoning: Concise, technical summary that states the bug (outbound replies ignore mediaMaxMb), root cause (loadWebMedia lacked maxBytes), and the fix (thread mediaMaxBytes through delivery chain), making it ideal for deduplication.

results.title: fix(telegram): add HTML parse fallback for media captions

summary.problem_summary: Telegram media sends (photo, document, video, animation, audio, voice, video note) could fail with a 400 "can't parse entities" error when captions contained invalid HTML entities because the media send path did not use the HTML-parse fallback used by text messages.
summary.solution_summary: Wrap the media send callback in withTelegramHtmlParseFallback so that on an HTML parse error the send is retried without parse_mode and with the raw caption (plain text); this change applies to all media types and leaves caption-splitting and other logic unchanged.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Small, backward-compatible bug fix in src/telegram/send.ts with a new unit test verifying the retry behavior; no config changes, text/edit paths unaffected, and the change can be reverted by restoring the send.ts modification.
summary.dedupe_summary: Telegram media caption HTML-parse errors cause 400 failures; add fallback to retry media sends as plain text without parse_mode.

judge.reasoning: Concise and specific: identifies HTML parse errors in Telegram media captions causing 400 failures and the added fallback retrying sends as plain-text without parse_mode, with no irrelevant boilerplate.

results.title: fix(whatsapp): make block streaming configurable and fix delivery race in block-reply pipeline

summary.problem_summary: WhatsApp had block streaming effectively disabled and a delivery race: block replies were enqueued optimistically and the pipeline marked content as sent before actual delivery, which could cause neither intermediate blocks nor the final reply to be delivered. A ternary bug also caused configured blockStreaming to be ignored (undefined vs false).
summary.solution_summary: Expose block streaming as channels.whatsapp.blockStreaming, fix process-message to correctly resolve the config, add sensible coalescing defaults, implement sendBlockReplyAsync to await confirmed delivery, and update onBlockReply to await that promise so sentContentKeys are only marked after success; tests updated accordingly.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Changes touch process-message.ts, reply-dispatcher.ts, dispatch-from-config.ts, shared.ts and tests; includes unit and manual verification showing both streaming-on and off behavior, and explicitly fixes the root cause rather than the prior workaround.
summary.dedupe_summary: WhatsApp block-streaming disabled/hardcoded and delivery race where optimistic enqueue suppressed final replies; needs configurable streaming and confirm-on-delivery for block replies.

judge.reasoning: Concise and specific summary that captures the core change (make WhatsApp block-streaming configurable) and the root cause (delivery race/optimistic enqueue suppressing final replies) without boilerplate, so it will cluster correctly with similar issues.

results.title: fix(signal): add group-level allowlist support via groups config

summary.problem_summary: Signal channel lacked a group-level allowlist option, so administrators could not whitelist specific Signal groups by ID to bypass sender-level groupAllowFrom checks or allow groups to run control commands, unlike other channels (Telegram, WhatsApp, etc.).
summary.solution_summary: Add channels.signal.groups.<groupId> config with a SignalGroupConfig type and Zod schema, wire resolveChannelGroupPolicy into the Signal event handler to grant group-level bypass of sender-level checks, and incorporate groupExplicitlyAllowed into commandGate authorizers; update docs and types accordingly.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: PR is safe to merge per review: TypeScript compilation passes, manually tested on a live Signal instance, addresses Greptile feedback by including group-level authorization in command gating, and preserves backward compatibility via hasGroupAllowFrom handling (confidence reported 5/5).
summary.dedupe_summary: Add group-level allowlist support for Signal (channels.signal.groups.<groupId>) to bypass sender-level groupAllowFrom checks and permit control commands for explicitly allowed groups.

judge.reasoning: Concise and specific: it states the core change (new channels.signal.groups.<groupId> allowlist), its effect (bypass sender-level groupAllowFrom) and purpose (permit control commands for allowed groups), with no boilerplate or noise.

results.title: fix(signal): add group-level allowlist support via groups config

summary.problem_summary: Signal channel lacked group-level allowlisting via channels.signal.groups.<groupId>, so when groupPolicy was set to "allowlist" and groupAllowFrom listed group IDs, group messages were silently dropped because groupAllowFrom is a sender-level filter and never matches group IDs.
summary.solution_summary: Add a groups config to SignalAccountSchemaBase (same shape as other channels) and call resolveChannelGroupPolicy in Signal's group access check before falling back to sender-level groupAllowFrom; add four tests to cover group policy resolution.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Small, targeted fix (label: channel: signal, size: S) that adds schema and event-handler logic; all relevant tests (config, Signal monitor, group-policy) pass and four new tests added; fixes #25540.
summary.dedupe_summary: Signal missing groups config / resolveChannelGroupPolicy caused allowlist group messages to be dropped; add group-level allowlist support.

judge.reasoning: Concise technical summary that identifies Signal lacked a groups config causing group-level allowlist checks to fail and states the fix to add group-level allowlist support.

results.title: feat(whatsapp): support config-driven block streaming

summary.problem_summary: WhatsApp channel hardcodes disableBlockStreaming:true in process-message.ts, so when agents send multiple messages with tool calls/sleeps in between all text is coalesced into one final WhatsApp message; delivery logic also incorrectly treated only queuedFinal as marking a reply delivered, ignoring intermediate blocks.
summary.solution_summary: Make WhatsApp honor channels.whatsapp.blockStreaming by adding resolveWhatsAppBlockStreamingEnabled and replacing the hardcoded flag with the config value, fix delivery counting so intermediate blocks can count as delivered, register blockStreaming capability and default in dock, and update tests accordingly.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Small, backward-compatible change with updated tests that aligns WhatsApp behavior with Telegram/Line; labeled for whatsapp-web, web-ui and agents and ready for review.
summary.dedupe_summary: WhatsApp always coalesces message blocks due to hardcoded disableBlockStreaming; change to config-driven blockStreaming so intermediate blocks are delivered separately.

judge.reasoning: Concise and specific: it identifies the hardcoded disableBlockStreaming causing WhatsApp to coalesce message blocks and states the change to make block streaming config-driven so intermediate blocks are delivered separately.
